content.full_text:
Dear Mr. Francis:
This letter is in response to your request for assistance regarding the provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
Fnrn Poltucal Pnacucns CouvtlssloN
P.O. Box 807 . '128 J Street . Sacramento. CA 9-5812-0807
t9t6).lll--r(160 . Fax (916) 322-08tt6
No','ember 8,2000
Mr. Grover Francis
6543 Milnes Road
Modesto, CA 95357
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our tr'ile No. A-00-231
Is a $250 voucher you received from an airline for being bumped from a flight
considered a gift to a public official or inappropriate to accept from the airline?
CONCLUSION
Because the $250 voucher you received for being bumped from a flight is a
discount made by the airline in the regular course of business to members of the public, it
does not constitute a gift under the Act.
FACTS
You are a director with an irrigation district in California. You recently traveled
to Salt Lake City for a conference. When returning, the flight was overbooked, and the
airline asked for volunteers to be bumped. You, along with your travel partners
volunteered. The airline gave each traveler who gave up his or her seat a $250 voucher
for their inconvenience.
I Government Code sections 81000 -91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
\


File No. A-00-231
Page No. 2
ANALYSN
Under section 89503, elected offrcers of a local govenrment agency are prohibited
from accepting gifts totaling more than $300 from any single source in a calendar year.
(Section 89503(a), (f); regulation 18940.2(a).) Elected officers and designated
employees are also required to report on an annual statement of economic interests gifts
aggregating $50 or more in value from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 87200
et seq.; 87207(a).) As an elected director with the Oakdale Irrigation District, you are
subject to these provisions.
Section 82028 of the Act defines "gift" as follows:
"(a) 'Gift' means, except as provided in subdivision (b), any payment that
confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration
ofequal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or discount
in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
fficial status. ..." (Emphasis added.)
We have advised previously that an official who receives a free ticket or pius
under an airline's frequent flyer program, available to all members of the public, has not
received a gift and is not required to disclose the free airline ticket or piNS on his or her
statement of economic interests. (Ackerman Advice Letter, No. A-97-044; Odom Advice
Letter, No. I-91-280.) In those cases, the airlines offered their frequent flyer program of
free tickets and arurual passes in the regular course ofbusiness to all members of the
public.
Similarly here, the airline has a policy of offering travel vouchers to customers
who agree to be bumped off overbooked flights. Because the airline offers these travel
vouchers in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
official status, the travel voucher you received from the airline does not constifute a gift
under section 82028 of the Political Reform Act. The travel voucher is not subject to the
Act's gift limit and you do not need to report it on your statement of economic interests.2
In addition, note that Article XII, Section 7, of the California Constitution, states
that "[a] transportation company may not grant free passes or discounts to anyone holding
an office in this state; and the acceptance of a pass or discount by a public officer, other
than a Public Utilities Commissioner, shall work a forfeiture of that offrce." I have
enclosed an excerpt from the booklet Conflicts of Interest, published by the Attorney
2 Our advice is limited to whether the travel voucher constitutes a reportable gift under the
Political Reform Act. As noted in the Ackerman Advice Letter, suprq, we do not advise whether the
officials may keep free tickets or tavel vouchers earned while on offrcial tavel, as this may be the subject
of agency personnel rules. You may wish to check with your agency's personnel officer, city attomey or
county counsel concerning these rules.


File \lo. A-00-231
Page No. 3
General's office in 1998, that discusses the constitutional prohibition and opinions issued
by the Attorney General's office interpreting the prohibition. You should contact the
Attorney General's office if you want further information about this constitutional
provision.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
f : !,,-\ "
By: HylaP. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
IIPWje

discount made by the airline in the regular course of business to members of the public, it
does not constitute a gift under the Act.

\

General's office in 1998, that discusses the constitutional prohibition and opinions issued
